name: Corossol / Annona Muricata
alt_text: A rectangular stamp with a warm red-orange background displaying a soursop fruit (corossol) in cross-section at left, showing pale yellow-green flesh with seeds, and a whole knobby green fruit at right; vertical text reading "REPUBLIQUE FEDERALE DU CAMEROUN" and "POSTES" runs along the left edge in black capitals; "COROSSOL ANNONA MURICATA" appears in small black type at lower center; the denomination "3F" is printed in black at upper left, and artist credits "P. LAMBERT" and "DELRIEU" appear at bottom.
description:
The verdant representation of the corossol on this three-franc stamp reflects the aesthetic language of 1960s francophone African postal design—a confident embrace of modernist botanical illustration combined with the vibrant color saturation that defined the era's Commonwealth and African postal systems. The composition is direct and uncluttered: a cross-section and whole fruit against a warmer background, rendered with naturalistic precision yet clearly stylized for legibility at small scale. This design approach positioned the stamp simultaneously as a scientific document and a celebration of regional agricultural wealth, a visual strategy common to African postal practice of the period.

The corossol, or soursop—scientifically Annona muricata—is a tropical fruit native to the Americas that spread throughout the Old World Tropics beginning in the 17th century. By the mid-twentieth century it had become widely cultivated across West and Central Africa, where it held both culinary and economic significance. The fruit's presence on the stamp—rendered with scientific accuracy yet framed as an object of aesthetic value—exemplified a broader postcolonial trend: reframing local and adopted agricultural products as sources of cultural and economic identity rather than colonial resource extraction.

Cameroon in 1967 was a nation consolidating its identity as a unified federal state. President Ahmadou Ahidjo, having taken office in 1960, was progressively centralizing power—a process that would culminate in the constitutional amendments of 1969 and the formal transition to a unitary state in 1972. Across postcolonial Africa in this era, postal administrations had begun deliberately showcasing national flora, fauna, and economic resources on stamps as visible assertions of sovereignty and national wealth. Cameroon's choice to issue a fruit series fit within this broader trend of African postal imagery, presenting local agricultural abundance to both domestic and international audiences. Whether the postal service explicitly intended the corossol series as part of this nation-building project remains undocumented, but the timing and subject matter align with how newly independent African nations were using stamps to assert their place in the world.
